The Importance of Being Earnest
A Trivial Comedy for Serious People
by Oscar Wilde

THE PERSONS IN THE PLAY

John Worthing, J.P.
Algernon Moncrieff
Rev. Canon Chasuble, D.D.
Merriman, Butler
Lane, Manservant
Lady Bracknell
Hon. Gwendolen Fairfax
Cecily Cardew
Miss Prism, Governess

THE SCENES OF THE PLAY

ACT I. Algernon Moncrieff's Flat in Half-Moon Street, W.
ACT II. The Garden at the Manor House, Woolton.
ACT III. Drawing-Room at the Manor House, Woolton.

FIRST ACT

SCENE

Morning-room in Algernon's flat in Half-Moon Street. The room is
luxuriously and artistically furnished. The sound of a piano is
heard in the adjoining room.

[Lane is arranging afternoon tea on the table, and after the music
has ceased, Algernon enters.]

ALGERNON. Did you hear what I was playing, Lane?

LANE. I didn't think it polite to listen, sir.

ALGERNON. I'm sorry for that, for your sake. I don't play
accurately--any one can play accurately--but I play with wonderful
expression. As far as the piano is concerned, sentiment is my
forte. I keep science for Life.

LANE. Yes, sir.

ALGERNON. And, speaking of the science of Life, have you got the
cucumber sandwiches cut for Lady Bracknell?

LANE. Yes, sir. [Hands them on a salver.]

ALGERNON. [Inspects them, takes two, and sits down on the sofa.]
Oh! . . . by the way, Lane, I see from your book that on
Thursday night, when Lord Shoreman and Mr. Worthing were dining
with me, eight bottles of champagne are entered as having been
consumed.

LANE. Yes, sir; eight bottles and a pint.

ALGERNON. Why is it that at a bachelor's establishment the
servants invariably drink the champagne? I ask merely for
information.

LANE. I attribute it to the superior quality of the wine, sir. I
have often observed that in married households the champagne is
rarely of a first-rate brand.

ALGERNON. Good heavens! Is marriage so demoralising as that?

LANE. I believe it IS a very pleasant state, sir. I have had very
little experience of it myself up to the present. I have only been
married once. That was in consequence of a misunderstanding between
myself and a young person.

ALGERNON. [Languidly.] I don't know that I am much interested in
your family life, Lane.

LANE. No, sir; it is not a very interesting subject. I never think
of it myself.

ALGERNON. Very natural, I am sure. That will do, Lane, thank you.

LANE. Thank you, sir. [Lane goes out.]

ALGERNON. Lane's views on marriage seem somewhat lax. Really, if
the lower orders don't set us a good example, what on earth is the
use of them? They seem, as a class, to have absolutely no sense of
moral responsibility.

[Enter Lane.]

LANE. Mr. Ernest Worthing.

[Enter Jack.]

[LANE goes out.]

ALGERNON. How are you, my dear Ernest? What brings you up to town?

JACK. Oh, pleasure, pleasure! What else should bring one anywhere?
Eating as usual, I see, Algy!

ALGERNON. [Stiffly.] I believe it is customary in good society to
take some slight refreshment at five o'clock. Where have you been
since last Thursday?

JACK. [Sitting down on the sofa.] In the country.

ALGERNON. What on earth do you do there?

JACK. [Pulling off his gloves.] When one is in town one amuses
oneself. When one is in the country one amuses other people. It is
excessively boring.

ALGERNON. And who are the people you amuse?

JACK. [Airily.] Oh, neighbours, neighbours.

ALGERNON. Got nice neighbours in your part of Shropshire?

JACK. Perfectly horrid! Never speak to one of them.

ALGERNON. How immensely you must amuse them! [Goes over and takes
sandwich.] By the way, Shropshire is your county, is it not?

JACK. Eh? Shropshire? Yes, of course. Hallo! Why all these cups?
Why cucumber sandwiches? Why such reckless extravagance in one so
young? Who is coming to tea?

ALGERNON. Oh! merely Aunt Augusta and Gwendolen.

JACK. How perfectly delightful!

ALGERNON. Yes, that is all very well; but I am afraid Aunt Augusta
won't quite approve of your being here.

JACK. May I ask why?

ALGERNON. My dear fellow, the way you flirt with Gwendolen is
perfectly disgraceful. It is almost as bad as the way Gwendolen
flirts with you.

JACK. I am in love with Gwendolen. I have come up to town
expressly to propose to her.

ALGERNON. I thought you had come up for pleasure? . . . I call
that business.

JACK. How utterly unromantic you are!

ALGERNON. I really don't see anything romantic in proposing. It is
very romantic to be in love. But there is nothing romantic about a
definite proposal. Why, one may be accepted. One usually is, I
believe. Then the excitement is all over. The very essence of
romance is uncertainty. If ever I get married, I'll certainly try
to forget the fact.

JACK. I have no doubt about that, dear Algy. The Divorce Court was
specially invented for people whose memories are so curiously
constituted.

ALGERNON. Oh! there is no use speculating on that subject.
Divorces are made in Heaven-- [Jack puts out his hand to take a
sandwich. Algernon at once interferes.] Please don't touch the
cucumber sandwiches. They are ordered specially for Aunt Augusta.
[Takes one and eats it.]

JACK. Well, you have been eating them all the time!

ALGERNON. That is quite a different matter. She is my aunt.
[Takes plate from below.] Have some bread and butter. The bread
and butter is for Gwendolen. Gwendolen is devoted to bread and
butter.

JACK. [Advancing to table and helping himself.] And very good
bread and butter it is too.

ALGERNON. Well, my dear fellow, you need not eat as if you were
going to eat it all. You behave as if you were married to her
already. You are not married to her already, and I don't think you
ever will be.

JACK. Why on earth do you say that?

ALGERNON. Well, in the first place girls never marry the men they
flirt with. Girls don't think it right.

JACK. Oh, that is nonsense!

ALGERNON. It isn't. It is a great truth. It accounts for the
extraordinary number of bachelors that one sees all over the place.
In the second place, I don't give my consent.

JACK. Your consent!

ALGERNON. My dear fellow, Gwendolen is my first cousin. And before
I allow you to marry her, you will have to clear up the whole
question of Cecily. [Rings bell.]

JACK. Cecily! What on earth do you mean? What do you mean, Algy,
by Cecily! I don't know any one of the name of Cecily.

[Enter Lane.]

ALGERNON. Bring me that cigarette case Mr. Worthing left in the
smoking-room the last time he dined here.

LANE. Yes, sir. [Lane goes out.]

JACK. Do you mean to say you have had my cigarette case all this
time? I wish to goodness you had let me know. I have been writing
frantic letters to Scotland Yard about it. I was very nearly
offering a large reward.

ALGERNON. Well, I wish you would offer one. I happen to be more
than usually hard up.

JACK. There is no good offering a large reward now that the thing
is found.

[Enter Lane with the cigarette case on a salver. Algernon takes it
at once. Lane goes out.]

ALGERNON. I think that is rather mean of you, Ernest, I must say.
[Opens case and examines it.] However, it makes no matter, for,
now that I look at the inscription inside, I find that the thing
isn't yours after all.

JACK. Of course it's mine. [Moving to him.] You have seen me with
it a hundred times, and you have no right whatsoever to read what
is written inside. It is a very ungentlemanly thing to read a
private cigarette case.

ALGERNON. Oh! it is absurd to have a hard-and-fast rule about what
one should read and what one shouldn't. More than half of modern
culture depends on what one shouldn't read.

JACK. I am quite aware of the fact, and I don't propose to discuss
modern culture. It isn't the sort of thing one should talk of in
private. I simply want my cigarette case back.

ALGERNON. Yes; but this isn't your cigarette case. This cigarette
case is a present from some one of the name of Cecily, and you
said you didn't know any one of that name.

JACK. Well, if you want to know, Cecily happens to be my aunt.

ALGERNON. Your aunt!

JACK. Yes. Charming old lady she is, too. Lives at Tunbridge
Wells. Just give it back to me, Algy.

ALGERNON. [Retreating to back of sofa.] But why does she call
herself little Cecily if she is your aunt and lives at Tunbridge
Wells? [Reading.] 'From little Cecily with her fondest love.'

JACK. [Moving to sofa and kneeling upon it.] My dear fellow, what
on earth is there in that? Some aunts are tall, some aunts are not
tall. That is a matter that surely an aunt may be allowed to
decide for herself. You seem to think that every aunt should be
exactly the same height! That is absurd. For heaven's sake give me
back my cigarette case. [Follows Algernon round the room.]

ALGERNON. Yes. But why does your aunt call you her uncle? 'From
little Cecily to her dear Uncle Jack.' There is no objection, I
admit, to an aunt being a small aunt, but why an aunt, no matter
what her size may be, should call her own nephew her uncle, I
can't quite make out. Besides, your name isn't Jack at all; it is
Ernest.

JACK. It isn't Ernest; it's Jack.

ALGERNON. You have always told me it was Ernest. I have introduced
you to every one as Ernest. You answer to the name of Ernest. You
look as if your name was Ernest. You are the most earnest-looking
person I ever saw in my life. It is perfectly absurd your saying
that your name isn't Ernest. It's on your cards. Here is one of
them. [Taking it from case.] 'Mr. Ernest Worthing, B. 4, The
Albany.' I'll keep this as a proof that your name is Ernest if
ever you attempt to deny it to me, or to Gwendolen, or to any one
else. [Puts the card in his pocket.]

JACK. Well, my name is Ernest in town and Jack in the country, and
the cigarette case was given to me in the country.

ALGERNON. Yes, but that does not account for the fact that your
small Aunt Cecily, who lives at Tunbridge Wells, calls you her
dear nephew. Come, old boy, you had much better have the thing out
at once.

JACK. My dear Algy, you talk exactly as if you were a dentist. It
is very vulgar to talk like a dentist when one isn't a dentist. It
produces a false impression.

ALGERNON. Well, that is exactly what dentists always do. They
always talk exactly as if they were not dentists. Now, go on! Tell
me the whole thing. I may mention that I have always suspected you
of being a confirmed and secret Bunburyist; and I am quite sure of
it now.

JACK. Bunburyist? What on earth do you mean by a Bunburyist?

ALGERNON. I'll reveal to you the meaning of that incomparable
expression as soon as you are kind enough to inform me why you are
Ernest in town and Jack in the country.

JACK. Well, produce my cigarette case first.

ALGERNON. Here it is. [Hands cigarette case.] Now produce your
explanation, and pray make it improbable. [Sits on sofa.]

JACK. My dear fellow, there is nothing improbable about my
explanation at all. In fact it's perfectly ordinary. Old Mr.
Thomas Cardew, who adopted me when I was a little boy, made me in
his will guardian to his grand-daughter, Miss Cecily Cardew.
Cecily, who addresses me as her uncle from motives of respect that
you could not possibly appreciate, lives at my place in the
country under the charge of her admirable governess, Miss Prism.

ALGERNON. And where is that place in the country, by the way?

JACK. That is nothing to you, dear boy. You are not going to be
invited . . . I may tell you candidly that the place is not in
Shropshire.

ALGERNON. I suspected that, my dear fellow! I have Bunburyed all
over Shropshire on two separate occasions. Well, go on. Why are
you Ernest in town and Jack in the country?

JACK. My dear Algy, I don't know whether you will be able to
understand my real motives. You are hardly serious enough. When
one is placed in the position of guardian, one has to adopt a very
high moral tone on all subjects. It's one's duty to do so. And as
a high moral tone can hardly be said to conduce very much to
either one's health or one's happiness, in order to get up to town
I have always pretended to have a younger brother of the name of
Ernest, who lives in the Albany, and gets into the most dreadful
scrapes. That, my dear Algy, is the whole truth pure and simple.

ALGERNON. The truth is rarely pure and never simple. Modern life
would be very tedious if it were either, and modern literature a
complete impossibility!

JACK. That wouldn't be at all a bad thing.

ALGERNON. Literary criticism is not your forte, my dear fellow.
Don't try it. You should leave that to people who haven't been at
a University. They do it so well in the daily papers. What you
really have to do is to get rid of your brother. I don't care much
for the name Ernest. I think it sounds a little too much. But for
a brother named Jack, I have the strongest contempt. Jack is not a
good name. It is a very bad name. In fact, it is not a name at
all. It is merely a designation. It is what everybody calls
everybody who isn't anybody. You have no brother of the name of
Jack, so you haven't got a brother at all. You are not a
Bunburyist, you are a Bunbury. But to return to Cecily. Is she
pretty?

JACK. She is only just eighteen, and I am forty-one. I need hardly
tell you that the fact of my being so much older than she is, is
the only thing that makes me hesitate about asking her to be my
wife.

ALGERNON. I don't think you will hesitate about that, old boy.
Girls like to be asked.

JACK. But, my dear fellow, I am her guardian. She is a ward. I
cannot ask her to marry me without first getting her guardian's
consent.

ALGERNON. You are her guardian. Give your consent.

JACK. It would be a gross dereliction of duty on my part. I should
be showing a very bad example to my ward. I don't think it would
be at all right.

ALGERNON. My dear fellow, you must marry her. She is a very pretty
girl, and I have no doubt she will make you a most excellent wife.

JACK. Algy, you are perfectly right. I shall propose to her the
moment I see her. But I don't see how I can get out of this
Bunbury business. What am I to do?

ALGERNON. You must get rid of your brother Ernest. He is not at
all necessary. You have been very useful to him, and he has been
of no use to you whatsoever. You can tell me everything about him.
I shall see him constantly. I can tell Gwendolen about him. I'll
make him a perfect paragon of propriety.

JACK. You can't do that. I have the strongest possible objections
to Ernest. He is my brother. I am devoted to him. I don't wish to
part with him. I love him. And you propose to tell Gwendolen about
him! I won't have it. I absolutely won't have it.

ALGERNON. My dear fellow, you must tell her. You can't possibly
keep it from her. Gwendolen is my first cousin, and I have always
been taught that a first cousin is a near relative, but a
Bunburyist is a person one can trust with one's secrets.

JACK. I don't believe you are right about Bunburying. I think it
is a most useful invention. I have been Bunburying all my life.

ALGERNON. Yes, and you have been a perfect Bunburyist. You have
always pretended to have a brother named Ernest. Now, I have a
sister called Cecily. We are both Bunburyists. I am a Bunburyist
in town, and you are a Bunburyist in the country. I think that is
very neat.

JACK. I don't think it is neat at all. It is perfectly ridiculous.

ALGERNON. Oh, it's a fine thing to be in earnest. It's a fine
thing to be in earnest about anything. I am not sure that I
entirely approve of this Bunburying. That is why I want to know
more about it. But, my dear fellow, you really must marry Cecily.
She is a very pretty girl. She is the prettiest girl I ever saw.
I am quite sure she will make you a most excellent wife.

JACK. I don't want to marry her. I don't want to marry anybody.

ALGERNON. Oh, you must marry her. You have always told me that you
were in love with her.

JACK. I have always told you that I was in love with Gwendolen. I
have always told you that I was going to propose to Gwendolen. I
am going to propose to her today.

ALGERNON. Oh, that is all right. You can propose to Gwendolen. I
think you had better. In fact, I know you had better. But you must
marry Cecily as well.

JACK. I won't do anything of the kind.

ALGERNON. Then you are a Bunburyist, and you have been trying to
deceive me. I knew it all along. I felt it in my bones. But I
shall expose you. I shall tell Gwendolen.

JACK. You can't do that. Gwendolen is my first cousin.

ALGERNON. That makes no difference. She is my first cousin, too.
I shall tell her that you are a Bunburyist.

JACK. You can't do that.

ALGERNON. I can, and I will.

JACK. Then I shall tell her about your sister Cecily.

ALGERNON. I don't think you will. I don't think you will be so
mean as that. You wouldn't be so mean. You know how sensitive I am
about that. I think you had better let me have the whole story.

JACK. I have told you the whole story.

ALGERNON. I don't believe you.

JACK. Then I shall go.

ALGERNON. No, you won't. You will stay here and have tea. You
always have tea at this hour, and Gwendolen will be here in a
minute.

JACK. I am going to see Gwendolen.

ALGERNON. You are not going anywhere until you have had tea. I
want to hear all about Cecily.

JACK. I have told you all about Cecily.

ALGERNON. You haven't told me what she looks like.

JACK. She is the prettiest girl I ever saw.

ALGERNON. Yes, but is she clever?

JACK. She is thoroughly practical.

ALGERNON. I want to know something more than that. I want to know
something about her appearance.

JACK. She has never been married.

ALGERNON. I don't see what that has to do with it.

JACK. It has a great deal to do with it, I can assure you. A girl
who is married is not so pretty as a girl who is not married.

ALGERNON. But that is not the point. I want to know what she looks
like.

JACK. She has golden hair and a sweet expression.

ALGERNON. That sounds rather disappointing.

JACK. She has a lovely complexion.

ALGERNON. I am not so sure about that.

JACK. She has the most wonderful eyes.

ALGERNON. What colour are they?

JACK. I don't know. I never noticed.

ALGERNON. You must have noticed the colour of her eyes. A man
always notices the colour of a girl's eyes.

JACK. I don't know what colour they are. I think they are blue.

ALGERNON. Then I am sure they are not blue. A man would never
notice the colour of a girl's eyes if they were blue.

JACK. I think you are very rude.

ALGERNON. I am not rude. I am merely trying to find out something
about Cecily.

JACK. Well, I can tell you anything you want to know about
Cecily.

ALGERNON. What is her income?

JACK. I don't know.

ALGERNON. What is her family?

JACK. I don't know.

ALGERNON. Where does she live?

JACK. I don't know.

ALGERNON. You don't know anything about her, do you?

JACK. I know that she is the prettiest girl I ever saw.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she is thoroughly practical.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she has golden hair and a sweet expression.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she has a lovely complexion.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she has the most wonderful eyes.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she is eighteen years old.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she is my ward.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that I am going to marry her.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she is the most beautiful girl in the world.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that I love her.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she will make me happy.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that I shall never be happy without her.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that I shall propose to her today.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that she will accept me.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that we shall be married next month.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that we shall live happily ever after.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that I am the happiest man in the world.

ALGERNON. But you don't know anything else about her?

JACK. I know that you are a nuisance.

ALGERNON. I know that you are a Bunburyist.

JACK. I am not a Bunburyist.

ALGERNON. You are a Bunburyist.

JACK. I am not.

ALGERNON. You are.

JACK. I am not.

ALGERNON. You are.

JACK. I am not.

ALGERNON. You are.

[Enter Lane.]

LANE. Lady Bracknell and Miss Fairfax.

[Enter Lady Bracknell and Gwendolen.]

[Lane goes out.]

ALGERNON. Aunt Augusta, how delightful!

LADY BRACKNELL. Algy, you are not looking at all well. You are
looking very pale. You look as if you had been leading a double
life. I hope you are not Bunburying.

ALGERNON. I have been Bunburying all day, aunt.

LADY BRACKNELL. I am sorry to hear that. You should not Bunbury
on weekdays. It is very bad for one's health. I am always telling
Gwendolen that she must not Bunbury. I hope she doesn't. I am
sure she doesn't. She has far too much sense. Gwendolen, have you
been Bunburying?

GWENDOLEN. No, aunt, I have been in town all day.

LADY BRACKNELL. That is very satisfactory. I am glad to hear it.
I hope you have not been talking to any one improper.

GWENDOLEN. I have been talking to Mr. Worthing.

LADY BRACKNELL. Mr. Worthing! I am not sure that I approve of
Mr. Worthing. I have not yet decided whether he is a Bunburyist
or not. I have asked him to come and see me. I shall question him
myself.

ALGERNON. I am sure he is not a Bunburyist, aunt.

LADY BRACKNELL. I hope not. I have the strongest possible
objections to Bunburying. I think it is very wrong. I have known
several Bunburyists, and they have all been very wicked people.

ALGERNON. I think you are very hard on Bunburyists, aunt.

LADY BRACKNELL. I am not hard on Bunburyists. I am merely trying
to point out that Bunburying is a very dangerous amusement. I
have known several Bunburyists who have been very seriously
injured. One of them lost his life. It was a very sad case. He
was a most excellent young man, and he was very much attached to
me. But he was a Bunburyist, and he Bunburyed once too often. He
was killed by a fall from a horse.

ALGERNON. How very sad!

LADY BRACKNELL. It was very sad. I was very much attached to him.
But I am glad to say that he died doing his duty. He was a
Bunburyist, and he died Bunburying.

ALGERNON. I think that was very noble of him.

LADY BRACKNELL. It was very noble of him. I think that a
Bunburyist who dies Bunburying is a noble Bunburyist. I wish
there were more like him.

ALGERNON. I wish there were more like him.

LADY BRACKNELL. I wish there were more like him.

[Enter Jack.]

JACK. Lady Bracknell, I have come to tell you that I am in love
with Gwendolen, and I wish to marry her.

LADY BRACKNELL. Mr. Worthing, I am not sure that I approve of
you. I have not yet decided whether you are a Bunburyist or not.
I shall question you myself.

JACK. I am not a Bunburyist, Lady Bracknell.

LADY BRACKNELL. I am glad to hear that. I have the strongest
possible objections to Bunburying. I think it is very wrong.

JACK. I quite agree with you, Lady Bracknell.

LADY BRACKNELL. I am glad to hear that. Now, tell me about your
family.

JACK. I have no family, Lady Bracknell.

LADY BRACKNELL. No family! That is very serious. I am not sure
that I approve of a man who has no family. I think it is very
wrong. A man should have a family.

JACK. I was found in a handbag, Lady Bracknell.

LADY BRACKNELL. In a handbag! How very extraordinary! I have
never heard of such a thing. I am not sure that I approve of a
man who was found in a handbag. I think it is very wrong. A man
should not be found in a handbag.

JACK. It was a very large handbag, Lady Bracknell.

LADY BRACKNELL. That makes no difference. A handbag is a
handbag. I am not sure that I approve of you, Mr. Worthing.

JACK. I hope you will, Lady Bracknell.

LADY BRACKNELL. I shall think about it. I shall think about it
very seriously. I shall let you know my decision.

[Exit Lady Bracknell.]

GWENDOLEN. Mr. Worthing, I have something very particular to say
to you.

JACK. I am all attention, Miss Fairfax.

GWENDOLEN. Mr. Worthing, I am going to ask you a very serious
question.

JACK. I am ready to answer it.

GWENDOLEN. What is your name?

JACK. My name is Ernest.

GWENDOLEN. I knew it was Ernest. I felt it in my bones. I knew
your name was Ernest the moment I saw you. You look exactly like
an Ernest. You have the most Ernest expression I ever saw. I am
so glad your name is Ernest.

JACK. I am so glad you like my name.

GWENDOLEN. I think it is a beautiful name. It is the most
beautiful name in the world.

JACK. I think so too.

GWENDOLEN. I am so glad we agree about that.

JACK. I am so glad we agree about everything.

GWENDOLEN. I am so glad we agree about that.

JACK. I am so glad we agree about everything.

[Enter Algernon.]

ALGERNON. Gwendolen, Aunt Augusta wants to see you.

GWENDOLEN. I must go, Mr. Worthing. I shall see you later.

[Exit Gwendolen.]

ALGERNON. Well, my dear fellow, have you proposed to Gwendolen?

JACK. Yes, I have.

ALGERNON. And what did she say?

JACK. She said she would marry me.

ALGERNON. How delightful! I am so glad.

JACK. But there is a difficulty.

ALGERNON. What is the difficulty?

JACK. Lady Bracknell does not approve of me.

ALGERNON. Oh, that is a difficulty.

JACK. She does not approve of my name.

ALGERNON. What is wrong with your name?

JACK. She thinks it is not a good name.

ALGERNON. What does she want you to change it to?

JACK. She wants me to change it to Ernest.

ALGERNON. But your name is Ernest.

JACK. Yes, but she doesn't know that.

ALGERNON. You must tell her.

JACK. I can't tell her.

ALGERNON. Why not?

JACK. Because she would not approve of Bunburying.

ALGERNON. Oh, I see.

JACK. What am I to do?

ALGERNON. You must become Ernest.

JACK. How can I become Ernest?

ALGERNON. You must be christened.

JACK. But I have already been christened.

ALGERNON. Then you must be christened again.

JACK. But that would be very inconvenient.

ALGERNON. It would be very inconvenient, but it would be very
necessary.

JACK. I shall think about it.

ALGERNON. You had better think about it quickly. Aunt Augusta is
coming back.

[Enter Lady Bracknell.]

LADY BRACKNELL. Mr. Worthing, I have thought about it, and I have
decided that I cannot approve of you.

JACK. But why, Lady Bracknell?

LADY BRACKNELL. Because your name is not Ernest.

JACK. But I can change my name.

LADY BRACKNELL. I am glad to hear that. You must change it at
once.

JACK. I shall change it today.

LADY BRACKNELL. You must change it before you marry Gwendolen.

JACK. I shall change it before I marry Gwendolen.

LADY BRACKNELL. That is very satisfactory. I am glad to hear it.

[Exit Lady Bracknell.]

ALGERNON. Well, my dear fellow, you have got out of that very
well.

JACK. Yes, I have.

ALGERNON. But what are you going to do about Cecily?

JACK. I don't know what to do about Cecily.

ALGERNON. You must tell her about Gwendolen.

JACK. I can't tell her about Gwendolen.

ALGERNON. Why not?

JACK. Because she would not understand.

ALGERNON. You must make her understand.

JACK. How can I make her understand?

ALGERNON. You must explain it to her.

JACK. I can't explain it to her.

ALGERNON. Why not?

JACK. Because I don't understand it myself.

ALGERNON. Oh, I see.

[Enter Lane.]

LANE. Miss Cardew.

[Enter Cecily.]

[Exit Lane.]

CECILY. Uncle Jack! Oh, I am so glad to see you!

JACK. My dear Cecily!

CECILY. I have come up to town to see you.

JACK. How delightful!

CECILY. I have something very particular to say to you.

JACK. I am all attention.

CECILY. Uncle Jack, I am in love.

JACK. My dear Cecily, you are too young to be in love.

CECILY. I am eighteen, Uncle Jack.

JACK. You are too young to be in love.

CECILY. I am not too young to be in love.

JACK. Who are you in love with?

CECILY. I am in love with Ernest.

JACK. Ernest! Who is Ernest?

CECILY. Ernest. Your brother Ernest.

JACK. My brother Ernest!

CECILY. Yes, your brother Ernest. I have never met him, but I am
in love with him.

JACK. But you can't be in love with a man you have never met.

CECILY. I can. I am.

JACK. But you don't know anything about him.

CECILY. I know that he is your brother.

JACK. But that is not enough.

CECILY. It is enough for me.

JACK. But you don't know what he looks like.

CECILY. I know that he is very handsome.

JACK. But you don't know that.

CECILY. I do know that. I have seen his photograph.

JACK. His photograph!

CECILY. Yes, his photograph. You gave me his photograph.

JACK. I gave you his photograph!

CECILY. Yes, you gave me his photograph. You said it was a
photograph of your brother Ernest.

JACK. But I don't have a brother Ernest.

CECILY. You do have a brother Ernest. You told me about him.

JACK. But I was telling you a lie.

CECILY. A lie!

JACK. Yes, a lie.

CECILY. Then you don't have a brother Ernest?

JACK. No, I don't have a brother Ernest.

CECILY. Then who gave me the photograph?

JACK. I don't know.

CECILY. You must know.

JACK. I don't know.

CECILY. You must know.

JACK. I don't know.

CECILY. You must know.

[Enter Algernon.]

ALGERNON. My dear Cecily!

CECILY. Ernest! Oh, I am so glad to see you!

ALGERNON. My dear Cecily, I am so glad to see you!

CECILY. I have been waiting for you.

ALGERNON. I have been waiting for you.

CECILY. I have been waiting for you for a very long time.

ALGERNON. I have been waiting for you for a very long time.

CECILY. I am so glad you are here.

ALGERNON. I am so glad you are here.

CECILY. I have something very particular to say to you.

ALGERNON. I have something very particular to say to you.

CECILY. I am in love with you.

ALGERNON. I am in love with you.

CECILY. I have been in love with you for a very long time.

ALGERNON. I have been in love with you for a very long time.

CECILY. I am so glad we love each other.

ALGERNON. I am so glad we love each other.

[Enter Jack.]

JACK. Cecily! What are you doing here?

CECILY. I am talking to Ernest.

JACK. But that is not Ernest.

CECILY. It is Ernest.

JACK. It is not Ernest. It is Algernon.

CECILY. Algernon!

JACK. Yes, Algernon.

CECILY. But he said he was Ernest.

ALGERNON. I am Ernest.

JACK. You are not Ernest. You are Algernon.

ALGERNON. I am Ernest.

JACK. You are not Ernest. You are Algernon.

ALGERNON. I am Ernest.

JACK. You are not Ernest. You are Algernon.

[Enter Gwendolen.]

GWENDOLEN. Ernest! Oh, I am so glad to see you!

JACK. My dear Gwendolen!

GWENDOLEN. I have been waiting for you.

JACK. I have been waiting for you.

GWENDOLEN. I have something very particular to say to you.

JACK. I have something very particular to say to you.

GWENDOLEN. I am in love with you.

JACK. I am in love with you.

GWENDOLEN. I have been in love with you for a very long time.

JACK. I have been in love with you for a very long time.

GWENDOLEN. I am so glad we love each other.

JACK. I am so glad we love each other.

CECILY. Uncle Jack! What is the meaning of this?

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

CECILY. You said you were not in love with Gwendolen.

JACK. I am not in love with Gwendolen.

CECILY. But you just said you were.

JACK. I was telling a lie.

CECILY. A lie!

JACK. Yes, a lie.

GWENDOLEN. Ernest! What is the meaning of this?

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

GWENDOLEN. You said you were not in love with Cecily.

JACK. I am not in love with Cecily.

GWENDOLEN. But you just said you were.

JACK. I was telling a lie.

GWENDOLEN. A lie!

JACK. Yes, a lie.

ALGERNON. My dear fellow, you really must tell the truth.

JACK. I am telling the truth.

ALGERNON. You are not telling the truth.

JACK. I am telling the truth.

ALGERNON. You are not telling the truth.

JACK. I am telling the truth.

ALGERNON. You are not telling the truth.

[Enter Miss Prism.]

MISS PRISM. Cecily! What are you doing here?

CECILY. I am talking to Ernest.

MISS PRISM. But that is not Ernest.

CECILY. It is Ernest.

MISS PRISM. It is not Ernest. It is Algernon.

CECILY. Algernon!

MISS PRISM. Yes, Algernon.

CECILY. But he said he was Ernest.

ALGERNON. I am Ernest.

MISS PRISM. You are not Ernest. You are Algernon.

ALGERNON. I am Ernest.

MISS PRISM. You are not Ernest. You are Algernon.

ALGERNON. I am Ernest.

MISS PRISM. You are not Ernest. You are Algernon.

[Enter Canon Chasuble.]

CANON CHASUBLE. What is the meaning of this?

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

CANON CHASUBLE. You must know what the meaning of this is.

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

CANON CHASUBLE. You must know what the meaning of this is.

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

CANON CHASUBLE. You must know what the meaning of this is.

[Enter Lane.]

LANE. Lady Bracknell.

[Enter Lady Bracknell.]

LADY BRACKNELL. What is the meaning of this?

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

LADY BRACKNELL. You must know what the meaning of this is.

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

LADY BRACKNELL. You must know what the meaning of this is.

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

LADY BRACKNELL. Mr. Worthing, I have made my decision. I cannot
approve of you.

JACK. But why, Lady Bracknell?

LADY BRACKNELL. Because you were found in a handbag.

JACK. But I can explain that.

LADY BRACKNELL. I don't want to hear your explanation.

JACK. But Lady Bracknell--

LADY BRACKNELL. I have made my decision. I cannot approve of you.

[Exit Lady Bracknell.]

JACK. This is terrible!

ALGERNON. I think it is very amusing.

JACK. It is not amusing. It is terrible.

ALGERNON. I think it is very amusing.

JACK. It is not amusing. It is terrible.

ALGERNON. I think it is very amusing.

JACK. It is not amusing. It is terrible.

[Enter Miss Prism.]

MISS PRISM. Mr. Worthing, I have something very particular to say
to you.

JACK. I am all attention.

MISS PRISM. Mr. Worthing, I am the person who found you.

JACK. You are the person who found me!

MISS PRISM. Yes, I am the person who found you.

JACK. Where did you find me?

MISS PRISM. I found you in a handbag.

JACK. In a handbag!

MISS PRISM. Yes, in a handbag.

JACK. Where was the handbag?

MISS PRISM. The handbag was in a cloakroom at Victoria Station.

JACK. At Victoria Station!

MISS PRISM. Yes, at Victoria Station.

JACK. But how did I get into the handbag?

MISS PRISM. I put you there.

JACK. You put me there!

MISS PRISM. Yes, I put you there.

JACK. But why did you put me there?

MISS PRISM. I put you there by mistake.

JACK. By mistake!

MISS PRISM. Yes, by mistake.

JACK. But how could you make such a mistake?

MISS PRISM. I was very young at the time.

JACK. But that is no excuse.

MISS PRISM. I know it is no excuse.

JACK. But you must have known what you were doing.

MISS PRISM. I did know what I was doing.

JACK. Then why did you do it?

MISS PRISM. I was very young at the time.

JACK. But that is no excuse.

MISS PRISM. I know it is no excuse.

[Enter Canon Chasuble.]

CANON CHASUBLE. Miss Prism, I have something very particular to
say to you.

MISS PRISM. I am all attention.

CANON CHASUBLE. Miss Prism, I am in love with you.

MISS PRISM. Canon Chasuble!

CANON CHASUBLE. Yes, Miss Prism, I am in love with you.

MISS PRISM. But you are a clergyman.

CANON CHASUBLE. I know I am a clergyman.

MISS PRISM. Then you cannot be in love with me.

CANON CHASUBLE. But I am in love with you.

MISS PRISM. But you cannot be in love with me.

CANON CHASUBLE. But I am in love with you.

MISS PRISM. But you cannot be in love with me.

CANON CHASUBLE. But I am in love with you.

[Enter Lady Bracknell.]

LADY BRACKNELL. What is the meaning of this?

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

LADY BRACKNELL. You must know what the meaning of this is.

JACK. I don't know what the meaning of this is.

LADY BRACKNELL. Mr. Worthing, I have changed my decision. I
approve of you.

JACK. You approve of me!

LADY BRACKNELL. Yes, I approve of you.

JACK. But why?

LADY BRACKNELL. Because I have just discovered that you are my
nephew.

JACK. Your nephew!

LADY BRACKNELL. Yes, my nephew. You are the son of my poor
sister, Mrs. Moncrieff.

JACK. Mrs. Moncrieff!

LADY BRACKNELL. Yes, Mrs. Moncrieff. She was my sister. She
married Mr. Moncrieff. They had two sons. One was called Ernest.
The other was called Algernon.

JACK. But I am not Ernest.

LADY BRACKNELL. No, you are not Ernest. You are Algernon.

JACK. But I am not Algernon.

LADY BRACKNELL. Then who are you?

JACK. I am Jack.

LADY BRACKNELL. Jack!

JACK. Yes, Jack.

LADY BRACKNELL. But that is not a name.

JACK. I know it is not a name.

LADY BRACKNELL. Then what is your real name?

JACK. My real name is Ernest.

LADY BRACKNELL. Ernest!

JACK. Yes, Ernest.

LADY BRACKNELL. Then you are my nephew.

JACK. Yes, I am your nephew.

LADY BRACKNELL. Then I approve of you.

JACK. Thank you, Lady Bracknell.

LADY BRACKNELL. You are welcome.

[Enter Gwendolen.]

GWENDOLEN. Mother! What is the meaning of this?

LADY BRACKNELL. Gwendolen, I have good news for you. Mr.
Worthing is my nephew.

GWENDOLEN. Your nephew!

LADY BRACKNELL. Yes, my nephew. He is the son of my poor sister,
Mrs. Moncrieff.

GWENDOLEN. Then he is Ernest!

LADY BRACKNELL. Yes, he is Ernest.

GWENDOLEN. Then I can marry him!

LADY BRACKNELL. Yes, you can marry him.

GWENDOLEN. Oh, I am so glad!

[Enter Cecily.]

CECILY. Uncle Jack! What is the meaning of this?

JACK. Cecily, I have good news for you. I am really Ernest.

CECILY. You are really Ernest!

JACK. Yes, I am really Ernest.

CECILY. Then I can marry you!

JACK. Yes, you can marry me.

CECILY. Oh, I am so glad!

[Enter Algernon.]

ALGERNON. My dear fellow, what is the meaning of this?

JACK. Algy, I have good news for you. You are really Algernon.

ALGERNON. I am really Algernon!

JACK. Yes, you are really Algernon.

ALGERNON. Then I can marry Cecily!

JACK. Yes, you can marry Cecily.

ALGERNON. Oh, I am so glad!

[Enter Canon Chasuble.]

CANON CHASUBLE. I have good news for you all. I am going to
marry Miss Prism.

MISS PRISM. Oh, I am so glad!

[Enter Lane.]

LANE. I have good news for you all. I am going to marry the
cook.

ALL. Oh, we are so glad!

[They all embrace.]

THE END
